lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent commercial farms that grow a combination of oilseeds and grains with no single crop family representing at least half of production value and repeatedly sell harvested crops or seed to external elevators, processors, merchandisers, cooperatives, or other customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Hobby and residential farms, passive farmland landlords, custom-farming contractors without crop ownership, captive production units, farms dominated by one oilseed or grain family and classified elsewhere, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and operations outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring annual commodity and seed sales, usually priced against transparent regional and global markets and delivered under spot, forward, hedged, cooperative, or production contracts; revenue varies with acres, yields, crop mix, quality, basis, and commodity prices.

a.bridge: USDA measures adoption of particular precision-agriculture tools and acreage, not the wage-weighted share of current not-yet-automated tasks in NAICS 111191. I translate widespread row-crop guidance, mapping, sensing, and variable-rate use into additional exposure for remaining equipment supervision, scouting, spraying, planting, harvesting, maintenance triage, crop planning, marketing, compliance, and administration, while discounting for already-automated guidance and for repair, field intervention, land stewardship, and accountable agronomic decisions. Acreage-weighted adoption may overstate exposure for smaller LMM farms and understate it for large commercial operators.
a.rationale: Row-crop production uses structured field routes, repeat machine operations, geospatial data, and standardized market and record workflows that are amenable to autonomy and AI decision support. Because automated guidance already covers more than half of major row-crop acreage, the estimate applies only to remaining tasks and does not count existing automation again.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory for commercial combination farms, linked to operator and hired-labor hours and measured pre/post changes from autonomous equipment, robotic scouting, AI agronomy, and office automation, would materially change the range.
caveats: USDA technology data are crop- and acreage-based, not specific to NAICS 111191 or the LMM EBITDA lens.
The latest crop-specific guidance adoption observations range from 2016 to 2019 and therefore do not measure the 2026 installed base.

rho.rationale: AI planning, recordkeeping, marketing support, scouting, variable-rate recommendations, and supervised autonomy can be implemented within five years, especially on scaled farms. Full field autonomy faces equipment-replacement cycles, interoperability and data-rights issues, rural connectivity, liability, safety around roads and people, weather variability, agronomic trust, and the short seasonal window for correcting failure.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of LMM corn-soy and mixed-grain farms reporting installed cost, acres covered, intervention frequency, downtime, yield and input effects, labor hours, insurance requirements, and payback would materially narrow the range.
caveats: Precision-tool adoption does not establish implementation of generative AI or driver-out autonomy.
Farm size strongly predicts adoption, but the frozen LMM population and equipment age are not observed.

e.rationale: Commercial combination farms generally own production risk and sell repeat crop output to external customers, fitting the operating lens. Eligibility is reduced for passive landlords, captive or affiliated operations, farms whose crop mix shifts them into another NAICS code, and businesses whose normalized EBITDA falls outside the band. No defensible frozen firm count exists, so this is a conditional share rather than evidence of acquisition volume.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level registry combining Census of Agriculture crop mix, ownership, related entities, gross cash farm income, normalized EBITDA, operator status, and external sales would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The frozen n is missing and must not be inferred from the much broader Census of Agriculture farm counts.
USDA reports 95% of all US farms are family-owned and operated, but that is not a direct eligibility rate for this six-digit LMM population.

s5.bridge: USDA's 2024 TOTAL survey measures planned farmland ownership transfers, not qualifying control transfers of NAICS 111191 operating companies, and the 2022 Census gives producer age and family ownership across all farms. I use the finding that about 5% of farmland acres are slated for sale or gift within five years as a floor-like anchor, then raise the central firm probability because the average producer age is 58.1 and a control transition can occur without selling all land. Family trusts, wills, gifts, partial operator changes, and lease turnover may not qualify, so acreage-transfer evidence can either understate business succession or overstate actionable third-party control sales.
s5.rationale: An aging, family-dominated population creates succession events, but farms commonly transfer gradually within families, retain land while changing operators, or move assets through trusts rather than sell an intact control-ready business. A 15% central qualifying-transfer probability recognizes turnover without equating land transition with an external control deal.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year operator-control transition data by farm size and commodity, plus lender, attorney, broker, and producer interviews on succession plans for LMM combination farms, would materially change the probability.
caveats: TOTAL covers all farmland and reports acres rather than operating-firm control events.
Family succession, trusts, gifts, land sales, and operator retirement often do not create a qualifying transferable company.

q.bridge: USDA observes long-run soybean productivity gains and projects abundant corn supplies and global competition constraining farm prices, not the five-year share of an implemented AI benefit retained by combination farms. I translate transparent commodity pricing and competitive yield diffusion into meaningful pass-through through lower prices, wider basis competition, higher land rents, and technology or input charges, while allowing temporary farm-level retention from proprietary execution, owned land, hedging, and locally superior yields. The proxy may understate retention during tight commodity cycles and overstate it for tenant operators whose rent resets quickly.
q.rationale: Individual farms are mostly price takers: once productivity diffuses, output expands and rents, equipment, seed, software, and input suppliers capture part of the gain. Early adopters can retain savings for several seasons, particularly on owned acreage and through better risk management, but a five-year central retained share below one half is prudent.
q.change_evidence: Farm-level panel data linking technology adoption to operating margins, cash rents, input and software fees, basis realization, hedging, and yields over renewal cycles would materially change retention.
caveats: No source directly measures AI-benefit sharing for mixed oilseed and grain farms.
Owned-versus-rented acres, crop marketing skill, and local land competition cause large retention differences.

d5.bridge: USDA's ten-year baseline forecasts acreage, production, and use for individual major crops rather than a five-year constant-quality demand ratio for NAICS 111191's crop basket. I interpolate its modest corn and soybean production and use growth over roughly half the projection window, while allowing crop switching and avoiding direct use of nominal receipts. This may overstate combination-farm demand if specialization increases or understate it if oilseed crush and exports grow faster than baseline.
d5.rationale: USDA expects major-row-crop acreage to soften but yields to lift production; corn use grows slightly less than 4% over ten years and soybean production ends at record levels. Food, feed, exports, crushing, and biofuel uses keep the physical basket durable, while global competition, conservation acres, policy, and crop substitution limit growth. Four percent central quantity growth over five years is consistent with a mature market.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year USDA baseline crosswalked to the actual revenue-weighted crop mix of NAICS 111191 farms, with domestic use, exports, crush, feed, seed, and biofuel volumes in constant-quality units, would materially change the ratio.
caveats: The USDA baseline assumes normal weather and current law and is not a forecast of cyclical shocks.
NAICS classification can change when one crop family exceeds half of a farm's production value, even if the farm remains operating.

o.rationale: Nearly all year-five demand for oilseeds and grains still requires an accountable farm operator to control land access, select crops, finance inputs, manage agronomic and weather risk, maintain equipment, comply with programs, market output, and bear yield and quality accountability. AI and autonomy can reduce labor but do not eliminate the operating enterprise; limited displacement could come from fully managed farming platforms, landlord or processor integration, or contract production that changes the operator form.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on acres operated under fully autonomous managed-farming services, processor or landlord integration, and control responsibility in equipment-as-a-service contracts would materially change the operator-required share.
caveats: The estimate distinguishes labor substitution from elimination of the accountable farm operator.
A change in legal or contractual operator form may remove a lens firm even though physical crop production continues.

narrative.executive_view: Oilseed and grain combination farming has substantial AI exposure in field operations, agronomy, and administration, but much precision automation is already installed and commodity economics limit retained value. Physical demand is mature and durable, with yields offsetting softer acreage. The decisive screen weakness is acquisition availability: the frozen labor share and firm count are both missing, and aging, family-owned farms often transition land or management internally rather than sell an intact control-ready company.
narrative.ai_impact: Auto-steer already covers more than half of major row-crop acreage, so the remaining opportunity is supervised-to-higher autonomy, robotic scouting, targeted spraying, sensor and imagery interpretation, predictive maintenance, crop and input planning, compliance, recordkeeping, and marketing support. Scaled farms adopt faster, but weather, agronomic edge cases, short planting and harvest windows, rural connectivity, interoperability, liability, and equipment cycles keep humans accountable.
narrative.value_capture: Early adopters can retain lower labor and input costs, better field timing, and yield gains for several seasons. Transparent commodity prices, global competition, higher output, cash-rent resets, and equipment, seed, input, and software pricing transfer much of the benefit over five years. Operators with owned land, distinctive execution, disciplined hedging, and less competitive rental markets should retain more than heavily rented price-taking farms.
narrative.firm_availability: No defensible n is available and it must remain missing. The broader farm sector is 95% family-owned and operated, with an average producer age of 58.1, but those facts do not establish a count of LMM combination farms or actionable sales. USDA expects only about 5% of farmland acres to move by sale or gift within five years, and family trusts, wills, leases, and gradual succession often separate land ownership from business control.
narrative.demand_durability: USDA's baseline projects slightly lower major-crop acreage but rising yields and production. Corn use grows modestly and soybean production reaches records, supported by feed, exports, crush, food, and industrial demand. Weather, trade, biofuel rules, global output, and crop prices create annual volatility, while the combination-farm classification itself can change with revenue mix even when the underlying operation persists.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Material risks include missing frozen l and n, commodity and basis volatility, drought and extreme weather, trade policy, crop insurance and farm-program changes, rising land and equipment costs, tenant concentration, data rights, equipment lock-in, input resistance, and succession complexity. Automation failures during narrow seasonal windows are costly. The evidence is mostly crop- or whole-farm-sector data rather than this exact six-digit LMM population.
narrative.principal_driver: Structured row-crop workflows and geospatial data support further autonomy and AI, while steady food, feed, crush, and export demand preserves the physical operating need.
narrative.principal_weakness: Both frozen opportunity denominators are missing, and family and land succession rarely maps cleanly to an actionable control transfer of an intact farm business.

sources.title: NAICS Sector 11 Definitions: 111191 Oilseed and Grain Combination Farming
sources.supports: Official scope: farms growing a combination of oilseeds and grains with no one oilseed or grain family accounting for half of agricultural production value.

sources.title: Precision Agriculture in the Digital Era: Recent Adoption on U.S. Farms
sources.supports: Digital-agriculture task and adoption evidence: substantial mapping and variable-rate use on corn and soybean acreage and automated guidance on well over half of major row-crop acreage.

sources.title: Largest farms most likely to adopt precision agriculture guidance systems
sources.supports: Size-based adoption proxy: guidance use reached 73% of the largest corn farms and 68% of the largest soybean farms, versus 10% and 11% among the smallest groups in the cited crop surveys.

sources.title: USDA releases 2022 Census of Agriculture data
sources.supports: Farm-structure proxy: 95% of US farms were family-owned and operated, average producer age was 58.1, 32% of farmland was in oilseed and grain specialization, and only 6% of farms had at least $1 million in sales.

sources.title: Most of the U.S. Rented Farmland is Owned by Non-Farmers
sources.supports: Transfer proxy from the 2024 TOTAL survey: about 43 million acres, roughly 5% of US farmland, are slated for ownership transfer by sale or gift within five years; only 23 million acres are expected to be sold to nonrelatives.

sources.title: USDA Agricultural Projections to 2035
sources.supports: Long-run demand and pricing proxy: lower major-crop acreage is offset by yield growth; corn production rises from 15.8 billion bushels in 2026/27 to nearly 16.6 billion in 2035/36, total corn use grows less than 4%, and soybean production ends at record levels.

sources.title: U.S. Soybean Production Expands Since 2002 as Farmers Adopt New Practices, Technologies
sources.supports: Productivity and commercial-retention proxy: from 2002 to 2022 soybean acreage rose 18% and yields 30%, while inflation-adjusted cost per bushel fell as technology and practices changed.
